Pandowdy Apple IIe Emulator
Table of Contents

Interface IFrameProvider

Namespace
Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces
Assembly
Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll

Provides access to the emulator's frame buffer for rendering with double-buffering support.

public interface IFrameProvider

Remarks

This interface manages the frame buffers used for rendering Apple IIe video output. It implements a double-buffering pattern where:

  • The back buffer is used for composing the next frame (writing)
  • The front buffer is used for display (reading)
  • Buffers are swapped atomically when the frame is complete
This prevents tearing and ensures smooth animation by keeping the display buffer stable while the next frame is being rendered. The FrameAvailable event notifies consumers (typically the UI layer) when a new frame is ready for display.

Properties

Height

Gets the height of the frame in scanlines.

int Height { get; }

Property Value

int

The frame height in scanlines. For Apple IIe, this is 192 scanlines (24 text rows × 8 scanlines per row).

IsGraphics

Gets or sets whether the emulator is in graphics mode (as opposed to text mode).

bool IsGraphics { get; set; }

Property Value

bool

True if displaying graphics (lo-res or hi-res); false if displaying text mode.

Remarks

This property reflects the state of the TEXT soft switch. When false (text mode), the display shows 40-column or 80-column text. When true (graphics mode), the display shows lo-res (40×48 color blocks) or hi-res (280×192 pixels) graphics. The primary purpose of this flag is to give the UI renderer a hint to use NTSC color generation and fringing when applicable.

IsMixed

Gets or sets whether the emulator is in mixed text/graphics mode.

bool IsMixed { get; set; }

Property Value

bool

True if displaying mixed mode (graphics on top 20 rows, text on bottom 4 rows); false if displaying pure text or pure graphics.

Remarks

This property reflects the state of the MIXED soft switch. When true, the top 160 scanlines display graphics content and the bottom 32 scanlines (4 text rows) display text. This mode is commonly used in Apple II games to show graphics with a text status line at the bottom. The primary purpose of this flag is to give the UI renderer a hint that the bottom 4 lines of the display are text and can optionally be excluded from NTSC color generation to reduce fringing of the text.

Width

Gets the width of the frame in pixels.

int Width { get; }

Property Value

int

The frame width in pixels. For Apple IIe standard modes, this is typically 560 pixels (280 pixels doubled for HGR, individual for DHGR, 14 (or 7) pixels per character × 40 (or 80) columns).

Remarks

Currently, this value is determined by the default implementation of BitmapDataArray. In a future refactoring, the frame provider may dictate dimensions at creation time to support additional graphics modes. The pixel width represents the final rendered output width, which may include horizontal doubling to achieve proper aspect ratio on square-pixel displays.

Methods

BorrowWritable()

Gets the back buffer for writing and composing the next frame.

BitmapDataArray BorrowWritable()

Returns

BitmapDataArray

A BitmapDataArray that can be written to for composing the next frame. This buffer is not visible to the display until CommitWritable() is called.

Remarks

This method is typically called by the rendering subsystem at the start of each frame generation cycle. The caller should render the complete frame into this buffer, then call CommitWritable() to make it visible. The term "Borrow" indicates that the caller has temporary exclusive access to this buffer for rendering purposes.

CommitWritable()

Swaps the front and back buffers and raises the FrameAvailable event.

void CommitWritable()

Remarks

This method atomically swaps the front and back buffers, making the newly rendered frame visible while providing the previous front buffer as the new back buffer for the next frame. After the swap, the FrameAvailable event is raised to notify the UI layer that a new frame is ready for display.

This method should be called by the rendering subsystem after completing a frame (typically once per VBlank period, approximately 60 times per second for Apple IIe).

GetFrame()

Gets the current front buffer for reading and displaying.

BitmapDataArray GetFrame()

Returns

BitmapDataArray

A BitmapDataArray containing the most recently committed frame, ready for display. This buffer is stable and will not change until the next call to CommitWritable().

Remarks

This method is typically called by the UI layer in response to the FrameAvailable event to retrieve the completed frame for display. The returned buffer should be treated as read-only to prevent race conditions with the rendering thread.

Events

FrameAvailable

Raised after a new frame has been committed and is available for display.

event EventHandler? FrameAvailable

Event Type

EventHandler

Remarks

This event is fired by CommitWritable() after the front and back buffers have been swapped. Subscribers (typically the UI layer) should respond by reading the front buffer via GetFrame() and updating the display. This event is typically raised at the video refresh rate (approximately 60 Hz for Apple IIe).
